questions.example_title: Combining Logarithms Using the Product Rule
questions.question_html: Express $\log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) + \log_4 k$ as a single logarithm.
questions.question_text: Express $\log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) + \log_4 k$ as a single logarithm.
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

Using the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows: \begin{align} \log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) + \log_4 k & = \bigl( \log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) \bigr) + \log_4 k \\[5pt] & = \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2\big) + \log_4 k\\[5pt] \end{align}

Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain \[ \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2\big) + \log_4 k = \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2 k\big). \]

Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get \begin{align} \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2 k \big) & = \log_{4}\big(k^2 k t t^2\big)\\[5pt] & = \log_{4}\big(k^{2+1} t^{1+2}\big)\\[5pt] & = \log_{4}\big(k^3 t^3\big). \end{align}

Therefore, \[ \log_{4}(k^2 t) + \log_{4}(t^2) + \log_4(k) = \log_{4}\big(k^3 t^3\big). \]
questions.explanation_text:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:
\[
\log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab)
\]
Using the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows:
\begin{align}
\log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) + \log_4 k
& = \bigl( \log_{4}\big(k^2 t\big) + \log_{4}\big(t^2\big) \bigr) + \log_4 k \\[5pt]
& = \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2\big) + \log_4 k\\[5pt]
\end{align}
Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain
\[
\log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2\big) + \log_4 k = \log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2 k\big).
\]
Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get
\begin{align}
\log_{4}\big(k^2 t t^2 k \big) & = \log_{4}\big(k^2 k t t^2\big)\\[5pt]
& = \log_{4}\big(k^{2+1} t^{1+2}\big)\\[5pt]
& = \log_{4}\big(k^3 t^3\big).
\end{align}
Therefore,
\[
\log_{4}(k^2 t) + \log_{4}(t^2) + \log_4(k) = \log_{4}\big(k^3 t^3\big).
\]

questions.example_title: Combining Logarithms Using the Product Rule
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

Using the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows: \begin{align} \log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq) & = \bigl( \log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) \bigr) + \log_3(pq)\\[5pt] & = \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)\\[5pt] \end{align}

Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain \[ \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)= \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2 pq\big). \]

Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get \begin{align} \log_{3} \big(p^3 q^2 pq\big) & = \log_{3} \big(p^3p q^2 q \big)\\[5pt] & = \log_{3} \big(p^{3+1} q^{2+1} \big)\\[5pt] & = \log_{3}\big(p^4 q^3\big). \end{align}

Therefore, \[ \log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq) = \log_{3}\big(p^4 q^3\big). \]
questions.explanation_text:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:
\[
\log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab)
\]
Using the product rule for logarithms, we can combine the first two terms as follows:
\begin{align}
\log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)
& = \bigl( \log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) \bigr) + \log_3(pq)\\[5pt]
& = \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)\\[5pt]
\end{align}
Using the product rule for logarithms once more, we obtain
\[
\log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2\big) + \log_3(pq)= \log_{3}\big(p^3 q^2 pq\big).
\]
Finally, if we use the commutative property of real numbers and the addition law for exponents, we get
\begin{align}
\log_{3} \big(p^3 q^2 pq\big)
& = \log_{3} \big(p^3p q^2 q \big)\\[5pt]
& = \log_{3} \big(p^{3+1} q^{2+1} \big)\\[5pt]
& = \log_{3}\big(p^4 q^3\big).
\end{align}
Therefore,
\[
\log_{3}\big(p^3\big) + \log_{3}\big(q^2\big) + \log_3(pq) = \log_{3}\big(p^4 q^3\big).
\]

questions.example_title: Combining Logarithms Using the Product Rule
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

Using the product rule for logarithms, the given expression can be rewritten as

\begin{align} \log(x+2) + \log(5x) & = \log\big( (x+2)\cdot 5x \big)\\[5pt] & = \log \left(5x^2 + 10x \right). \end{align}

questions.example_title: Combining Logarithms With Coefficients Using the Product Rule
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

If we factor the given expression and use the product rule for logarithms, we get

\begin{align} 4\log 2+4\log 4+4 \log 8 &= 4 \left(\log 2+4\log 4 + \log 8\right) \\[5pt] &= 4\log (2 \cdot 4 \cdot 8) \\[5pt] &= 4\log 64. \end{align}

Therefore, \[ 4\log 2+4\log 4 + 4\log 8 = 4\log 64. \]
questions.explanation_text:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:
\[
\log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab)
\]
If we factor the given expression and use the product rule for logarithms, we get
\begin{align}
4\log 2+4\log 4+4 \log 8
&= 4 \left(\log 2+4\log 4 + \log 8\right) \\[5pt]
&= 4\log (2 \cdot 4 \cdot 8) \\[5pt]
&= 4\log 64.
\end{align}
Therefore,
\[
4\log 2+4\log 4 + 4\log 8 = 4\log 64.
\]

questions.example_title: Separating a Logarithm Into Two Logarithms Using the Product Rule
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab) \]

Using the product rule, the given expression can be rewritten as \begin{align} \log_2(8 p) = \log_2 8 + \log_2 p. \end{align}

Since $8 = 2^3,$ we have \[ \log_2 8 = \log_2\left( 2^3 \right) = 3. \]

Therefore, \begin{align} \log_2(8 p) & = \log_2 8 + \log_2 p\\[5pt] & = 3 + \log_2 p. \end{align}
questions.explanation_text:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:
\[
\log_n a + \log_n b = \log_n(ab)
\]
Using the product rule, the given expression can be rewritten as
\begin{align}
\log_2(8 p) = \log_2 8 + \log_2 p.
\end{align}
Since $8 = 2^3,$ we have
\[
\log_2 8 = \log_2\left( 2^3 \right) = 3.
\]
Therefore,
\begin{align}
\log_2(8 p) & = \log_2 8 + \log_2 p\\[5pt]
& = 3 + \log_2 p.
\end{align}

questions.example_title: Evaluating an Expression Using the Product Rule
questions.explanation_html:
First, let's recall the product rule for logarithms:

\[ \log_n a + \log_n b= \log_n(ab) \]

Using the product rule for logarithms and the information given, we get

\begin{align} 2\log 64 & = 2\log(16\cdot 4)\\[5pt] &=2\bigl( \log 16 + \log 4 \bigr) \\[5pt] & = 2(x + y). \end{align}
questions.explanation_text:

\[
\log_n a + \log_n b= \log_n(ab)
\]
Using the product rule for logarithms and the information given, we get
\begin{align}
2\log 64 & = 2\log(16\cdot 4)\\[5pt]
&=2\bigl( \log 16 + \log 4 \bigr) \\[5pt]
& = 2(x + y).
\end{align}

tasks.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.explanation_html:
To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln \left( {3-7x} \right) &= 6 \\[3pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln \left( {3-7x} \right)} &=e^{\color{blue}6} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln(3-7x)} = 3-7x.$

Therefore, \begin{align*} 3-7x & = e^6 \\[3pt] - 7x &= e^6 - 3 \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{e^6 - 3}{ - 7} \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{3 -e^6}{7}. \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.explanation_html:
To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln\left( {4x} \right) &= 2 \\[3pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln\left( {4x} \right)} &=e^{\color{blue}2} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln (4x)} = 4x.$

Therefore,

\begin{align*} 4x &= e^{2} \\[5pt] x &= \dfrac{1}{4}e^{2}\\[5pt] x &= \dfrac{e^{2}}{4}. \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by First Rearranging the Equation
questions.explanation_html:
First, we rearrange our equation to isolate the logarithmic term, as follows:

\begin{align*} 5\ln (7-2x) +13 &= 38 \\[3pt] 5\ln (7-2x) &= 25 \\[3pt] \ln (7-2x) &= 5 \end{align*}

To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln{(7-2x)} &= 5 \\[3pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln{(7-2x)}} &=e^{\color{blue}5} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln (7-2x)} = 7-2x.$

Therefore,

\begin{align*} 7-2x &= e^5 \\[5pt] -2x &= e^5-7 \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{e^5-7}{-2} \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{7-e^5}{2}. \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by First Rearranging the Equation
questions.explanation_html:
First, we rearrange our equation to isolate the logarithmic term, as follows:

\begin{align*} 2\ln (1-5x) +3 &= 9 \\[3pt] 2\ln (1-5x) &= 6 \\[3pt] \ln (1-5x) &= 3 \end{align*}

To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln{(1-5x)} &= 3 \\[3pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln{(1-5x)}} &=e^{\color{blue}3} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln (1-5x)} = 1-5x.$

Therefore,

\begin{align*} 1-5x &= e^3 \\[5pt] -5x &= e^3-1 \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{e^3-1}{-5} \\[3pt] x &= \dfrac{1-e^3}{5}. \end{align*}

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by Combining Logarithmic Terms
questions.question_text: Find the values of $x$ such that $\ln(x-1) - 3\ln(x-1) = 2.$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we simplify our equation by collecting the like logarithmic terms, and isolating the logarithm, as follows:

\begin{align*} \ln(x-1) - 3\ln(x-1) &= 2 \\[5pt] (1-3)\ln(x-1) &=2 \\[5pt] -2\ln(x-1) &= 2 \\[5pt] \ln(x-1) &= -1 \end{align*}

To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln(x-1) &= -1 \\[5pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln(x-1)} &= e^{\color{blue}-1} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln (x-1)} = x-1.$

Therefore,

\begin{align*} x-1 &= e^{-1} \\[5pt] x-1 &= \dfrac{1}{e} \\[5pt] x & = \dfrac{1}{e} + 1. \end{align*}
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.

questions.topic_name: Solving Logarithmic Equations Containing the Natural Logarithm
questions.example_title: Solving Natural Logarithmic Equations by Combining Logarithmic Terms
questions.question_html: If $2\ln{x} + 3\ln{x}=20$, then what is the value of $x?$
questions.question_text: If $2\ln{x} + 3\ln{x}=20$, then what is the value of $x?$
questions.explanation_html:
First, we simplify our equation by collecting the like logarithmic terms, and isolating the logarithm, as follows: \begin{align*} 2\ln{x} + 3\ln{x} &= 20 \\[5pt] (2+3)\ln x &= 20 \\[5pt] 5\ln x &= 20 \\[5pt] \ln x &= \dfrac{20}{5}\\[5pt] \ln x &=4. \end{align*}

To remove the logarithm, we exponentiate both sides of the equation. The logarithm is a natural logarithm, which means its base is $e.$ So, we use a base of $e$ for the exponentiation as well:

\begin{align*} \ln x &= 4 \\[5pt] e^{\color{blue}\ln x} &= e^{\color{blue}4} \end{align*}

Since exponentiation is the inverse function of the logarithm, we get $e^{\ln x} = x.$

Therefore, $ x = e^{4}. $
questions.hint_status: No hint field is exposed by the Math Academy completed-task review API.
